channel.title: Nature
channel.description: Nature is the foremost international weekly scientific journal in the world and is the flagship journal for Nature Portfolio. It publishes the finest peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature publishes landmark papers, award winning news, leading comment and expert opinion on important, topical scientific news and events that enable readers to share the latest discoveries in science and evolve the discussion amongst the global scientific community.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Author Correction: Modelling late gastrulation in stem cell-derived monkey embryo models
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10700-7

Author Correction: Modelling late gastrulation in stem cell-derived monkey embryo models
item.dc:title: Author Correction: Modelling late gastrulation in stem cell-derived monkey embryo models

item.title: Meet the biologists deciphering marine-mammal histories from baleen, whiskers and tusks
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01622-5

The nascent field of wildlife endocrinology is upending scientists’ understanding of pregnancies and stress
item.dc:title: Meet the biologists deciphering marine-mammal histories from baleen, whiskers and tusks
item.dc:creator: Virginia Gewin

item.title: Memory on trial: the new science of when to trust eyewitness testimony
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01618-1

Innocent people are frequently convicted of crimes on the basis of a witness’s false memories. Emerging science poses a surprising solution.
item.dc:title: Memory on trial: the new science of when to trust eyewitness testimony
item.dc:creator: R. J. Mackenzie

item.title: Bottom-Up Synthesis of Molecular Nanodiamond from Nanographene
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10669-3

Bottom-Up Synthesis of Molecular Nanodiamond from Nanographene
item.dc:title: Bottom-Up Synthesis of Molecular Nanodiamond from Nanographene

item.title: Poland’s economy is thriving, but its science is dying
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01664-9

Poland’s economy is thriving, but its science is dying
item.dc:title: Poland’s economy is thriving, but its science is dying
item.dc:creator: Maria W. Górna
Michał Tomza
Agata Starosta
Łukasz Okruszek

item.title: Conservation gains should not be at the mercy of political changes
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01665-8

Conservation gains should not be at the mercy of political changes
item.dc:title: Conservation gains should not be at the mercy of political changes
item.dc:creator: Juan F. Ovalle
Dylan Craven

item.title: A cautious voice on the closure of China’s journal ranking list
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01663-w

A cautious voice on the closure of China’s journal ranking list
item.dc:title: A cautious voice on the closure of China’s journal ranking list
item.dc:creator: Xiaochuang Li
Wenhao Qian

item.title: Should there be a national museum of chemicals?
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01336-8

A proposal to highlight the work of chemists, and reflections on 150 years of London Zoo in this week’s pick from the Nature archive.
item.dc:title: Should there be a national museum of chemicals?

item.title: Iran’s Internet blackout: a scholar’s month in the dark
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01666-7

Iran’s Internet blackout: a scholar’s month in the dark
item.dc:title: Iran’s Internet blackout: a scholar’s month in the dark
item.dc:creator: Mohammad Sal Moslehian

item.title: Too dangerous to release: is Mythos the start of the restricted-AI era?
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01617-2

What happens when AI companies produce models that they say the public can’t have — and how should users and governments react?
item.dc:title: Too dangerous to release: is Mythos the start of the restricted-AI era?
item.dc:creator: Chris Stokel-Walker

item.title: Hard-to-detect mutations explain how common autoimmune diseases arise
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01415-w

An old theory gains fresh support from advanced DNA sequencing, which can detect rare mutations in B cells from people with autoimmune thyroid conditions.
item.dc:title: Hard-to-detect mutations explain how common autoimmune diseases arise
item.dc:creator: Sergei B. Koralov
Timothy C. Borbet

item.title: Robots run this laboratory in Japan — and are changing how scientists work
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01625-2

Researchers hope to build a facility with thousands of robots capable of performing experiments independently by 2040.
item.dc:title: Robots run this laboratory in Japan — and are changing how scientists work
item.dc:creator: Rachel Fieldhouse

item.title: Innovation starts in schools — lessons from China
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01620-7

Countries that want successful innovation systems must invest in science education and science teachers.
item.dc:title: Innovation starts in schools — lessons from China
item.dc:creator: Dan Tao
Rui Wei
Yonghe Zheng

item.title: Why AI can’t be trusted to write scientific reviews
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01616-3

The production of the highest-quality literature reviews requires the judgement and expertise of people.
item.dc:title: Why AI can’t be trusted to write scientific reviews
item.dc:creator: Rupa Sarkar

item.title: When the grid can’t keep up: how South African laboratories handle power outages
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00402-5

For most research facilities, long periods without electricity would be a disaster. For scientists across South Africa, it’s just another day.
item.dc:title: When the grid can’t keep up: how South African laboratories handle power outages
item.dc:creator: Max Bennett

item.title: How I eavesdrop on frog conversations
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01621-6

Billie Goolsby’s experience with hearing loss helped her to develop a robotic tadpole for deciphering the secret lives of amphibians.
item.dc:title: How I eavesdrop on frog conversations
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Preston

item.title: How to breathe life back into brain theory
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01619-0

Neuroscience needs to stop treating the brain as if it is a computer.
item.dc:title: How to breathe life back into brain theory
item.dc:creator: Àlex Gómez-Marín

item.title: Vanishing tongues and life on Mars: Books in brief
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01655-w

Andrew Robinson reviews five of the best science picks.
item.dc:title: Vanishing tongues and life on Mars: Books in brief
item.dc:creator: Andrew Robinson

item.title: Major Ebola outbreak is escalating: what happens next
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01660-z

Nature staff discuss ongoing efforts to curb the Bundibugyo virus outbreak.
item.dc:title: Major Ebola outbreak is escalating: what happens next
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Thompson
Rachel Fieldhouse

item.title: Neuroflix
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01087-6

It’s just a game — isn’t it?
item.dc:title: Neuroflix
item.dc:creator: John McLaughlin

item.title: AI cracks 80-year-old mathematics challenge — researchers are astonished
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01651-0

The late Hungarian mathematician Paul Erdős thought he had the last word on a geometry problem. Now an OpenAI chatbot has proved him wrong.
item.dc:title: AI cracks 80-year-old mathematics challenge — researchers are astonished
item.dc:creator: Davide Castelvecchi

item.title: Hit a lab project glitch? Thinking about your thesis title like a storyteller can help you focus
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01392-0

Cell biologist and crime novelist Frances Brodsky says writing fiction has taught her perseverance and improved her manuscripts.
item.dc:title: Hit a lab project glitch? Thinking about your thesis title like a storyteller can help you focus
item.dc:creator: Dom Byrne

item.title: Stress impairs your brain’s ability to link memories — dampening insight
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01644-z

Imaging suggests why the ability to make inferences declines after an episode of acute stress, such as a job interview.
item.dc:title: Stress impairs your brain’s ability to link memories — dampening insight
item.dc:creator: Simon Spichak

item.title: De novo design of miniproteins targeting GPCRs
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10656-8

De novo design of miniproteins targeting GPCRs
item.dc:title: De novo design of miniproteins targeting GPCRs

item.title: Did a boy’s life-saving gene therapy cause his brain tumour?
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01593-7

Genetic sleuthing uncovers a rare case of cancer caused by a virus administered as part of a child’s treatment for a genetic disorder.
item.dc:title: Did a boy’s life-saving gene therapy cause his brain tumour?

item.title: A star gone rogue tears through the Galaxy
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01591-9

The black hole at the Milky Way’s centre catapulted a Sun-like star to ‘hypervelocity’ speed.
item.dc:title: A star gone rogue tears through the Galaxy

item.title: Ebola outbreak spirals out of control: how might it have started?
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01645-y

Public-health researcher James Baguma has studied the interactions between bats, which can carry the virus, and people in the region near the latest epidemic.
item.dc:title: Ebola outbreak spirals out of control: how might it have started?
item.dc:creator: Mohana Basu

item.title: Should I get a dog? What to know about pet ownership as a scientist
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00198-4

Animal companions can be a lot of work, and are difficult to fit into a busy professional’s life, but they can also enhance well-being.
item.dc:title: Should I get a dog? What to know about pet ownership as a scientist
item.dc:creator: Hannah Docter-Loeb

item.title: See the clouds streaming and vanishing around this planet — 690 light years away
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01608-3

James Webb Space Telescope reveals weather patterns from how planet WASP-94 A b filters the light of its parent star.
item.dc:title: See the clouds streaming and vanishing around this planet — 690 light years away
item.dc:creator: Davide Castelvecchi

item.title: Ebola outbreak: the data that show why researchers are so alarmed
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01646-x

The size of the outbreak in its initial days is worrying researchers. The next few weeks will determine how large it grows, they say.
item.dc:title: Ebola outbreak: the data that show why researchers are so alarmed
item.dc:creator: Ewen Callaway
Mariana Lenharo
Lauren Wolf

item.title: How we’re using AI tools to improve psychedelic-drug research
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01467-y

Félix Schoeller’s team built a realistic artificial-intelligence chatbot to train facilitators needed for research into psychoactive drugs — and, ultimately, to improve public health.
item.dc:title: How we’re using AI tools to improve psychedelic-drug research
item.dc:creator: Robin Berghaus

item.title: Daily briefing: Wearable robot could help kids with neuromuscular disease stand
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01656-9

A robotic device helps children with spinal muscular atrophy build up strength in their knees. Plus, a tough peer-review process could lead to a more-cited paper and chemists’ mission to replace ‘forever chemicals’.
item.dc:title: Daily briefing: Wearable robot could help kids with neuromuscular disease stand
item.dc:creator: Jacob Smith

item.title: Spinal neuromotor rehabilitation using a portable isokinetic training robot
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10642-0

Spinal neuromotor rehabilitation using a portable isokinetic training robot
item.dc:title: Spinal neuromotor rehabilitation using a portable isokinetic training robot
item.dc:creator: Yuebing Li
Jiaxin Ren
Tony Shu
Fuzhen Yuan
Yanggang Feng

item.title: Author Correction: Inactivating SnRK1β1A promotes broad-spectrum disease resistance in rice
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10659-5

Author Correction: Inactivating SnRK1β1A promotes broad-spectrum disease resistance in rice
item.dc:title: Author Correction: Inactivating SnRK1β1A promotes broad-spectrum disease resistance in rice
item.dc:creator: Guixin Yuan
Xunli Lu
Xingbin Wang
Mengfei Li

item.title: Nearly half of the world’s Nature Index chemistry research is now done in China
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00431-0

A breakdown of leading countries and institutions highlights China’s dominance in the field.
item.dc:title: Nearly half of the world’s Nature Index chemistry research is now done in China

item.title: Red light therapy: the science behind the hype
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01643-0

From glowing red face masks to fibre optic needles, we explore the gap between commercial devices and the field of ‘photobiomodulation’.
item.dc:title: Red light therapy: the science behind the hype
item.dc:creator: Shamini Bundell
Maren Hunsberger

item.title: AI ‘scientists’ promise to accelerate research — how do they work?
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01627-0

Systems generate hypotheses and ways to test them — plus, how mobile phones might one day be able to see around corners.
item.dc:title: AI ‘scientists’ promise to accelerate research — how do they work?
item.dc:creator: Nick Petrić Howe
Benjamin Thompson

item.title: What China’s rise in chemistry means for the rest of the world
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00428-9

China now produces more than three times the United States’ chemistry output in the Nature Index, underscoring its rapidly growing influence in the field.
item.dc:title: What China’s rise in chemistry means for the rest of the world
item.dc:creator: Bec Crew

item.title: Cusp-singularity-enhanced Coriolis effect for sensitive chip-scale gyroscopes
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10565-w

By using singularity physics to enable cubic-root scaling of frequency and phase modulations induced by the Coriolis effect to enhance the performance of chip-scale Coriolis vibratory gyroscopes, substantial improvements in signal-to-noise ratio and precision are demonstrated.
item.dc:title: Cusp-singularity-enhanced Coriolis effect for sensitive chip-scale gyroscopes
item.dc:creator: Sen Zhang
Dingbang Xiao
Fei Wang
Ran Huang
Lei Yu
Ning Zhou
Kaixuan He
Xuezhong Wu
Franco Nori
Hui Jing
Xin Zhou

item.title: A pathogen lncRNA secreted into rice sequesters a host miRNA for virulence
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10572-x

A fungal long non-coding RNA from Magnaporthe oryzae translocates into rice cells to sequester a host microRNA that normally represses PKR1, a negative immunity regulator, thereby facilitating infection and revealing a widespread RNA-based pathogen–host interaction mechanism.
item.dc:title: A pathogen lncRNA secreted into rice sequesters a host miRNA for virulence
item.dc:creator: Min He
Jia Su
Xiaogang Zhou
Tuo Qi
Jiazheng Wang

Xuewei Chen

item.title: De novo design of quasisymmetric two-component protein cages
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10464-0

Researchers designed two-component proteins forming quasisymmetric cages via geometric frustration, enabling tunable virus-like assemblies for cargo delivery, cellular uptake and studying intracellular diffusion and protein localization.
item.dc:title: De novo design of quasisymmetric two-component protein cages
item.dc:creator: Shunzhi Wang
Ying Xie
David Chemielewski
Connor Weidle
Tong Shu
Green Ahn
Ryan D. Kibler

Evans Brackenbrough
Emily Joyce
Kejia Wu
Andrew J. Borst
Andrew Favor
Buwei Huang
Frank DiMaio
Liam J. Holt
David Baker

item.title: Genetic analysis of circulating metabolic traits in 619,372 individuals
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10532-5

A genome-wide association study combining data from the Estonian Biobank and the UK Biobank identifies many common and low-frequency locus–metabolic trait associations, enabling the identification of putative causal links with disease outcomes.
item.dc:title: Genetic analysis of circulating metabolic traits in 619,372 individuals
item.dc:creator: Ralf Tambets
Mihkel Jesse
Jaanika Kronberg
Adriaan van der Graaf
Erik Abner
Urmo Võsa
Ida Rahu
Nele Taba
Anastassia Kolde

Andres Metspalu

Krista Fischer
Zoltán Kutalik
Tõnu Esko
Kaur Alasoo
Priit Palta

item.title: Nonlinear atomic tunnelling boosted by bright squeezed vacuum
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10485-9

Bright squeezed vacuum light boosts nonlinear atomic tunnelling ionization more than 20-fold compared with coherent light, enabling quantum control of strong-field processes without increasing classical intensity.
item.dc:title: Nonlinear atomic tunnelling boosted by bright squeezed vacuum
item.dc:creator: Zhejun Jiang
Shengzhe Pan
Jianqi Chen
Mingyu Zhu
Chenhao Zhao
Yiwen Wang
Ru Zhang
Jianshi Lu
Lulu Han
Suwen Xiong
Dian Wu
Wenxue Li
Shicheng Jiang
Hongcheng Ni
Jian Wu

item.title: Dopamine drives persistent remodelling of the maternal brain
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10509-4

Brain-wide transcriptomic profiling in mice reveals that reproductive experience remodels the maternal brain by altering dopamine dynamics in the dorsal hippocampal formation, causing dopamine-dependent histone post-translational modifications, and thereby changes in gene expression and behaviour.
item.dc:title: Dopamine drives persistent remodelling of the maternal brain
item.dc:creator: Jennifer C. O’Chan
Giuseppina Di Salvo
Ashley M. Cunningham
Sohini Dutta
Elizabeth Brindley
Benjamin H. Weekley
Winnie Chen
Rasika R. Iyer
Ethan Wan
Cindy Zhang
Naguib Mechawar
Gustavo Turecki
Ian Maze

item.title: Feature-specific threat coding in lateral septum guides defensive action
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10520-9

The populations of CRHR2-expressing neurons in the lateral septum of mice involved in threat-related behaviour in response to environmental challenges are defined.
item.dc:title: Feature-specific threat coding in lateral septum guides defensive action
item.dc:creator: Dionnet Leandro Bhatti Mazo
Marc Z. C. Berger
Amanda Loren Pasqualini
Sherry Jingjing Wu
Christopher M. Reid
Salvador Ignacio Brito
Shenfeng Qiu
Pat Levitt
Todd Erryl Anthony
Gord Fishell

item.title: A critical initialization for biological neural networks
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10528-1

Basic properties of symmetric random matrices can explain the emergence of macroscopic patterns in neural networks, suggesting that initialization conditions of connected neural populations may confer computational advantages across the mouse brain.
item.dc:title: A critical initialization for biological neural networks
item.dc:creator: Marius Pachitariu
Lin Zhong
Alexa Gracias
Amanda Minisi
Crystall Lopez
Carsen Stringer

item.title: Neural representation of action symbols in primate frontal cortex
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10297-x

A drawing-like task designed to study compositional generalization identifies a specific neural population in the ventral premotor cortex in primates that encodes action symbols.
item.dc:title: Neural representation of action symbols in primate frontal cortex
item.dc:creator: Lucas Y. Tian
Kedar Garzón Gupta
Daniel J. Hanuska
Adam G. Rouse
Mark A. G. Eldridge
Marc H. Schieber
Xiao-Jing Wang
Joshua B. Tenenbaum
Winrich A. Freiwald

item.title: A deep-learning framework reveals whole-body perturbations at cell level
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10535-2

An analysis of diet-induced obesity using MouseMapper—a suite of foundation-model-based deep-learning algorithms—identifies structural alterations of the infraorbital branch of the trigeminal ganglia.
item.dc:title: A deep-learning framework reveals whole-body perturbations at cell level
item.dc:creator: Doris Kaltenecker
Izabela Horvath
Rami Al-Maskari
Ying Chen

Farida Hellal
Markus Elsner
Harsharan Singh Bhatia
Ingo Bechmann
Bjoern H. Menze
Stephan Herzig
Johannes Christian Paetzold
Mauricio Berriel Diaz
Ali Ertürk

item.title: Advancing solar and wind penetration in China through energy complementarity
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10570-z

Using high-resolution satellite imagery combined with a deep-learning-based framework to build a national energy inventory enables a data-driven assessment of solar–wind complementarity strategies to reduce power variability and enhance renewable energy penetration across China.
item.dc:title: Advancing solar and wind penetration in China through energy complementarity
item.dc:creator: Yuan Hu
Hou Jiang
Chuan Zhang
Jianlong Yuan
Mengting Zhang
Ling Yao
Qiang Chen
Jichao Wu
Hualong Zhang
Subin Ma
Xiang Li
Weiyu Zhang
Quanhua Dong
Congcong Wen
Gege Yin
Fan Zhang
Chaohui Yu
Zhijun Jin
Yu Liu

item.title: Imaging hidden objects with consumer LiDAR via motion-induced sampling
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10502-x

Researchers enable hidden-object imaging on consumer LiDAR by fusing multiple frames with a motion-based model, achieving three-dimensional reconstruction, tracking and localization using low-cost, off-the-shelf smartphone sensors.
item.dc:title: Imaging hidden objects with consumer LiDAR via motion-induced sampling
item.dc:creator: Siddharth Somasundaram
Aaron Young
Akshat Dave
Adithya Pediredla
Ramesh Raskar

item.title: Design of one-component quasisymmetric protein nanocages
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10554-z

Quasisymmetry could arise from spontaneous symmetry breaking in a system of strongly interacting building blocks with programmed curvatures, and this principle, coupled with a design approach, can generate a rich array of quasisymmetric assemblies.
item.dc:title: Design of one-component quasisymmetric protein nanocages
item.dc:creator: Sangmin Lee
David Chmielewski
Shunzhi Wang
Ryan D. Kibler
Jisu Shin
Ann Carr
Young-Jun Park
David Veesler
David Baker

item.title: Divergent urban storm response to convective, frontal and tropical systems
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10479-7

Analysis of five types of warm-season storms in Texas using three-dimensional radar reflectivity data shows that urbanization can influence storm frequency, intensity, or vertical distribution, depending on the storm type.
item.dc:title: Divergent urban storm response to convective, frontal and tropical systems
item.dc:creator: Xinxin Sui
John Nielsen-Gammon
Zong-Liang Yang
Dev Niyogi

item.title: Early fossil eukaryotes were benthic aerobes
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10533-4

Integrated palaeontological, sedimentological and geochemical analyses of ancient rocks from Australia show that early eukaryotes were largely restricted to oxygenated benthic habitats, probably possessed mitochondria by 1.75 billion years ago, and may have expanded into planktonic habitats much later, perhaps during the Neoproterozoic era.
item.dc:title: Early fossil eukaryotes were benthic aerobes
item.dc:creator: Maxwell A. Lechte
Leigh Anne Riedman
Susannah M. Porter
Galen P. Halverson
Margaret Whelan

item.title: A SAUR gene enhances maize drought resilience by promoting silk elongation
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10566-9

The Small Auxin Up RNA (SAUR) protein ZmSAUR72 in maize (Zea mays) promotes silk growth via regulation of H+-ATPase activity, and is a key determinant of the anthesis-silking interval and thus resilience to drought.
item.dc:title: A SAUR gene enhances maize drought resilience by promoting silk elongation
item.dc:creator: Chaohui Zhu
Zhirui Yang
Shiping Yang
Xueyan Zhou
Boxin Liu
Tian Tian
Bochen Zhao
Yingying Xie
Yujun Liu
Jinkui Cheng
Huaijun Tang
Yanjun Zhang
Xiaoqing Xie
Lei Zhang
Cheng Liu
Xingrong Wang
Shengxue Liu
Feng Qin

item.title: Mitochondrial l-2-hydroxyglutarate is a physiological signalling metabolite
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10564-x

l-2-Hydroxyglutarate is identified as a legitimate physiological signalling metabolite, and control of its levels is essential for postnatal growth and survival and correct renal development and function.
item.dc:title: Mitochondrial l-2-hydroxyglutarate is a physiological signalling metabolite
item.dc:creator: Ram P. Chakrabarty
Jonathan G. Van Vranken
Yuki Aoi
Taylor A. Poor
Gregory S. McElroy
Karthik Vasan
Shimaa H. A. Soliman
Marta Iwanaszko
Rogan A. Grant
Benjamin C. Howard
Colleen R. Reczek
Anjali D. Chandel
Michael Kahl
Zhaofa Xu
Kathryn A. Helmin
Qiushi Jin
Dongmei Wang
Peng Gao
Jenna L. E. Blum
Zachary L. Sebo
Feng Yue
Yongchao C. Ma
Shawn M. Davidson
Steven P. Gygi
Samuel E. Weinberg
Benjamin D. Singer
SeungHye Han
Ali Shilatifard
Navdeep S. Chandel

item.title: Forest carbon protocols underestimate climate-driven carbon loss risks
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10571-y

The buffer pool designed to compensate for unintended carbon losses from the largest forest climate mitigation programme in the United States is too small when considering the impact of future climate change scenarios.
item.dc:title: Forest carbon protocols underestimate climate-driven carbon loss risks
item.dc:creator: Chao Wu
Grayson Badgley
Michael L. Goulden
James T. Randerson
Anna T. Trugman
Jonathan A. Wang
Linqing Yang
Nezha Acil
Susan C. Cook-Patton
Danny Cullenward
Steven J. Davis
Christopher A. Williams
William R. L. Anderegg

item.title: Astrocyte glucocorticoid receptor signalling restricts neuronal plasticity
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10512-9

Combined single-cell transcriptomic and chromatin accessibility sequencing analysis of mouse primary visual cortex across postnatal development reveals that the glucocorticoid receptor drives astrocyte maturation to limit neuronal plasticity.
item.dc:title: Astrocyte glucocorticoid receptor signalling restricts neuronal plasticity
item.dc:creator: Bruno Gegenhuber
Takuma Sonoda
Lisa Traunmüller
Christopher P. Davis
Shon A. Koren
Eric C. Griffith
Chinfei Chen
Michael E. Greenberg

item.title: High-fidelity identification of guest species in porous materials
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10527-2

A reconstruction method based on Gaussian-apodized single-sideband electron ptychography removes artefacts to enable the high-fidelity identification of guest species in porous materials.
item.dc:title: High-fidelity identification of guest species in porous materials
item.dc:creator: Qilong Feng
Liang Wang
Yuanhao Li
Xiaoqiu Xu
Yuan Yao
Yue Liu
Yonghe Li
Tulai Sun
Changlin Zheng
Lina Li
Hui Jin
Chongzhi Zhu
Jia Zhao
Peng Guo
Xiaonian Li
Yu Han
Yihan Zhu

item.title: Monkeys that ‘draw’ reveal a neuronal population that encodes combinable actions
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00928-8

When faced with unfamiliar problems, humans and other animals must plan and execute action sequences that they might not have used before. In macaque monkeys, this ability is supported by a population of neurons in the brain’s frontal cortex that encode recombinable action units or ‘action symbols’.
item.dc:title: Monkeys that ‘draw’ reveal a neuronal population that encodes combinable actions

item.title: Becoming a mother leaves long-lasting molecular memories
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01327-9

In mice, motherhood induces transcriptional changes in the brain that endure beyond short-term hormonal shifts. Postpartum stress disrupts these patterns.
item.dc:title: Becoming a mother leaves long-lasting molecular memories
item.dc:creator: Frances A. Champagne

item.title: Why are PFASs so hard to replace?
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00429-8

As the toxic legacy of ‘forever chemicals’ grows, researchers are racing to develop safer alternatives.
item.dc:title: Why are PFASs so hard to replace?
item.dc:creator: Katharine Sanderson

item.title: Tough peer-review process? Your paper might end up being more highly cited
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01615-4

An AI-led analysis of publicly available peer-review reports links requests for major revisions with papers that end up having high impact.
item.dc:title: Tough peer-review process? Your paper might end up being more highly cited
item.dc:creator: Mariana Lenharo

item.title: Daily briefing: Bogus citations will get you banned from arXiv
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01648-9

arXiv’s stance on AI-hallucinated references is a good start, but not tough enough, say some researchers. Plus, the brain’s code seems to be in constant flux and how to learn to love networking.
item.dc:title: Daily briefing: Bogus citations will get you banned from arXiv
item.dc:creator: Flora Graham

item.title: The brain’s code seems to be in constant flux. Neuroscientists are baffled
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01554-0

Neurons fire much more erratically than researchers thought. What does that mean for how the brain works?
item.dc:title: The brain’s code seems to be in constant flux. Neuroscientists are baffled
item.dc:creator: Diana Kwon

item.title: The CAPTCHA protocol
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01085-8

Difficult choices.
item.dc:title: The CAPTCHA protocol
item.dc:creator: Shiao Wang

item.title: Cities affect small and large storms differently
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01323-z

Analysis of a 23-year record of Texan storms reveals how urban landscapes affect storm rainfall — painting a more complex picture than had been realized.
item.dc:title: Cities affect small and large storms differently
item.dc:creator: Wei Zhang

item.title: Wearable robot boosts strength of children with spinal muscular atrophy
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01573-x

The device helps muscle recovery in those receiving gene therapy for the rare neuromuscular condition.
item.dc:title: Wearable robot boosts strength of children with spinal muscular atrophy
item.dc:creator: Liam Drew

item.title: A guide to the Nature Index
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00432-z

A description of the terminology and methodology used in this supplement, and a guide to the functionality that is available free online at natureindex.com.
item.dc:title: A guide to the Nature Index

item.title: Too little or too much sleep is linked to faster ageing throughout the body
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01564-y

A multimodal analysis reveals a ‘U-shaped’ association between sleep duration and biological ageing across various organ systems, with too much or not enough sleep being linked with accelerated organ ageing. The study also shows that biological ageing differentially mediates the relationship between extremes in sleep duration and late-life depression.
item.dc:title: Too little or too much sleep is linked to faster ageing throughout the body

item.title: Three scientists pushing chemistry in new directions
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00430-1

From improving battery technology to speeding up reaction times, these innovative scientists are making their mark.
item.dc:title: Three scientists pushing chemistry in new directions
item.dc:creator: Sandy Ong
Felicity Nelson

item.title: NoTrue, Silence and Rubbish Communications: satirical journals give Chinese academics a pressure valve
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01548-y

Viral trend highlights frustration over the ‘credibility crisis’.
item.dc:title: NoTrue, Silence and Rubbish Communications: satirical journals give Chinese academics a pressure valve
item.dc:creator: Xiaoying You

item.title: A conference taught me that scientists and journalists must work together to protect research
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00824-1

Science sleuth Lonni Besançon realizes that he has sometimes misunderstood what the media want from him and his researcher colleagues.
item.dc:title: A conference taught me that scientists and journalists must work together to protect research
item.dc:creator: Lonni Besançon

item.title: Quantum light source boosts attosecond science
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01324-y

Ionization experiment shows that quantum light can behave like a conventional laser that has a higher intensity.
item.dc:title: Quantum light source boosts attosecond science
item.dc:creator: Jan Marcus Dahlström

item.title: A multi-agent system for automating scientific discovery
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 19 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10652-y

A multi-agent system for automating scientific discovery
item.dc:title: A multi-agent system for automating scientific discovery
item.dc:creator: Ali Essam Ghareeb
Benjamin Chang
Ludovico Mitchener
Angela Yiu
Caralyn J. Szostkiewicz
Dmytro Shved
